learning_outcomes.description: apply the ethical and legal aspects of conducting vulnerability assessment and penetration testing
interpret the processes of vulnerability assessment and penetration testing
select and apply suitable techniques and tools for reconnaissance, sniffing, scanning and enumeration
recognise the known vulnerabilities in existing protocols, systems and applications
design and compare suitable strategies and techniques for gaining access to target applications or systems
develop customised plans of vulnerability discovery, management and remediation

assessments.due_date:
Week 2 - Week 5

Week 7 - Week 13

Practical reports are submitted during the first hour of each student's allocated practical session.
assessments.task_description:
The weekly practical work will involve tasks and exercises to be progressively assessed each week during the student's allocated practical session. Weekly attendance at your allocated practical session is mandatory and is required to pass this course. Missed sessions are calculated as zero marks for that session.

Every practical session develops new skills and assesses the application of the skills from the previous week's practical session. Thus, practical sessions are cumulative, each week building on the previous week. The first Practical class in week 2 provides the formative learning that will be assessed the following week. All ten remaining practical sessions start with summative assessments of the previous week's practical skills.

Due to the nature of the labs (cyber attacks), these environments are isolated from external networks (no outbound traffic, no port forwarding, no VPNs) – technical issues if students attempt to remotely access the labs for assessment will not be considered as students are required to attend the EECS computer labs where the lab supervisors are present to handle any technical issues.

Every student has a unique assessment environment set up every week for their allocated Practical class. (Most formative lab environments are identical for every student.) Your unique environment to perform your assessment is only instantiated in the labs for the first hour of your allocated Practical class with immediate marking and feedback. After the first hour of every Practical class, the marking system automatically closes and changes over to set up for the following Practical class.

The practical sessions will be delivered in the Computer Labs in the General Purpose South building (78-116) or, for some weeks, in the UQ Cyber War Rooms on Level 4 of the General Purpose South building (78), all timetabling shows the location as 78-116. You may not attend another session that you are not allocated to. Lab sessions will be very full and the practical environments will be set up with the logins of the students allocated to each particular session in UQ's class allocation system.

The course uses only Unix-based tools and does not use Microsoft Windows or other GUI-based systems. All practical assessments use a shell command line (see Assumed Background). The prerequisite assumed knowledge will not be re-taught in this course.

Details of each weekly session's tasks and method for submission will be provided on the Lab Instructions each week (downloadable from Blackboard each week). There are no practical sessions in Week 1 or Week 6 (due to a public holiday affecting some of the classes in Week 6).

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The weekly practical reports must be submitted during the first hour of the student’s allocated practical session. Late submissions receive a 100% late penalty and no extensions are possible. Target machines are only orchestrated for each hour of your scheduled lab session and are torn-down automatically and changed to accommodate the next session. Information required for your unique solution for the weekly assessments can only be obtained during your practical session.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
This assignment requires students to compile a report that demonstrates the ability to locate high quality sources of relevant information, to understand complex concepts, to analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently, and the ability to synthesise a clear and concise report of the appropriate level and style. The specific details, tasks, and assessment criteria for this assignment will be posted to Blackboard after the first lecture. The assignment runs concurrently with the first half of the course's lectures and practical classes, but assesses a completely different aspect of the course objectives, requiring a combination of risk assessment, legal, and ethical considerations that you have researched after the Week 1 lecture, with a considerable portion of the marks for demonstrating correct academic referencing of both AI and non-AI sources. Your final report must then presented in a manner appropriate for the target audience.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The final exam will be a summative assessment which covers all topics in this course. 

The exam will be an on-campus paper-based exam. This exam will be closed-book and will contain a combination of problem-solving and short answer questions.

As the final exam (including any deferred or supplementary exams) are in-person paper-based secure exams with no electronic aids permitted, Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are not available to students while performing this assessment.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 13(23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic:
Lectures

Lectures will be mainly used to introduce new material. Learning materials are available on Blackboard.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Lectures Lectures will be mainly used to introduce new material. Learning materials are available on Blackboard.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 2 To Week 13(02 Mar - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic:
Practical lab sessions

Practical sessions are based on the topics covered by lectures. They will be mainly used to work on hands-on exercises and challenges. This will be a more interactive process than lecture presentations. The intent is to provide a learning environment that provides better support for developing practical skills. There will also be time during practical sessions specifically for getting help and being assessed by lab demonstrators.


Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
